source_metadata.title_raw: Peter J. Nolan - A-20-005 - February 11, 2020 - Palm Springs

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

February 11, 2020


Peter J. Nolan
SBEMF LLP
1800 E. Tahquitz Canyon Way
Palm Springs, CA 92262

Re:    Your Request for Advice
       Our File No. A-20-005

Dear Mr. Nolan:

       This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Mayor of the City of Needles,
Jeff Williams, regarding the gift and conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the
"Act").¹

       Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of
interest or Section 1090.

       Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1
FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is
not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for
additional advice.


                                          QUESTIONS

       1. May Mayor Jeff Williams accept donations to alleviate medical-related living costs from
Mr. Todd Kaplan or Mr. Drew Milburn, in excess of $500 under the limited exception to the gift
limits in Regulation 18942(a)(18)(B) for "Acts of Human Compassion?"

       2. If so, as a general matter, what are the Mayor's duties in the event either individual is
involved in a decision before the City Council?




_________________________
¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-20-005
Page No. 2

CONCLUSIONS

1. No. Under the facts provided, these individuals are connected to Mayor Williams through their business interests within the City, their business has had decisions before the City within the past 12 months, and it is anticipated that their business will have business permit and licensing issues before the City in the near future. Therefore, any such payments from the individuals would not meet the limited exception to the definition of a "gift," under Regulation 18942(a)(18)(B) for payments that are acts of human compassion unrelated to the official's status.

2. As a general matter, if Mr. Williams accepts gifts from either individual, the gift limits, reporting requirements, and conflict of interest prohibitions will apply, as discussed below. The Act prohibits Mayor Williams from accepting any gift over $500, as well as making, participating in making, or using his position to influence the outcome of a governmental decision involving the donor of a gift or gifts with an aggregate value of $500 or more provided to, received by, or promised to him within the 12 months prior to the date the decision is made.

FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER

Mayor Williams suffers from ongoing medical issues and is in need of a kidney transplant. He is retired and makes enough money to cover the bills for his home. Mayor Williams created a "GoFundMe" account to accept donations to help alleviate costs related to his medical needs. He has been advised of the $500 limit under Section 89503 and is aware of his recusal/disqualification requirements surrounding the acceptance of gifts, should any council business be impacted.

You clarified by email that Mayor Williams is seeking financial help specifically related to temporary housing after the transplant. He lives in the City of Needles and the transplant center is over 4 hours away. He will need to rent a place to live prior to the transplant (unknown for how long) and after the transplant, for at least 3 months. These costs will include living expenses for the months he is out of his home in Needles. The procedure itself is covered by Medicare; however, the living issue is not covered.

The Mayor has been a public official since April 2000. Todd Kaplan and Drew Milburn are part of a partnership group which owns Vertical Companies, a cannabis business which has operations in the City. Mayor Williams states that these are two "very good" friends, whom he has known for over five years. He describes them as "family." He met the individuals through a mutual friend, Senator Tony Strickland. They met Mayor Williams when they came to the City with a business idea within the City related to their cannabis business. Mr. Kaplan and Mr. Milburn do not currently have any business before the Council or the City, and both wish to give over the $500 limit to help alleviate the financial burden due to the Mayor's increasing medical-related costs.

You clarified in additional emails, that both of these individuals had zoning and conditional permit ("CUP") issues before the City in the past related to their business. The last matter before the City Council was in April 2019, where one of the men appeared on behalf of Vertical Companies. The City Manager states that Vertical Companies has been issued all of its necessary

File No. A-20-005
Page No. 3

land use permits, and it is not anticipated that their business will require further City Council
actions. However, their business will need building permits and a business license which are
administered by City staff.

ANALYSIS

A "gift" is a subcategory of "income" (Section 82030) under the Act, and is defined in
Section 82028(a) as:

… any payment that confers a personal benefit on the recipient, to the extent that
consideration of equal or greater value is not received and includes a rebate or
discount in the price of anything of value unless the rebate or discount is made in
the regular course of business to members of the public without regard to official
status.

The type of payment you describe from Mr. Kaplan or Mr. Milburn would qualify as a gift
to Mayor Williams so long as he is serving as a public official. To reduce improper influences on
public officials, the Act regulates the receipt of gifts by public officials in three ways:

• Gift Limits: The Act places limitations on the acceptance of gifts by certain
public officials. The current limit is $500, in aggregate, from a single source
in a calendar year. (Section 89503, Regulation 18940.2.) Gifts from lobbyists
or a lobbying firm that lobbies the official's agency may not aggregate to
more than $10 in a calendar month. (Section 86203.)

• Gift Reporting: Any gift of $50 or more (or gifts that aggregate to $50 or
more) from the same source received in a calendar year must be disclosed on
the officials' statements of economic interests. (Section 87207.)

• Conflict of Interest Disqualification: The Act prohibits any public official
from making, participating in making, or using his or her position to influence
the outcome of a governmental decision involving the donor of a gift or gifts
with an aggregate value of $500 or more provided to, received by, or
promised to the official within the 12 months prior to the date the decision is
made. (Sections 87100 and 87103(e).)

Unless the payments fall under an exception to the definition a "gift" the payments are
subject to the $500 limit, will require reporting at the $50 level, and may create a conflict of interest
in City decisions involving the individual's financial interests, including a business interest.

Gift Exceptions: Regulation 18942(a)(18)(B) Acts of Human Compassion

The Commission adopted an exception to the definition of "gift" and "income" where
charitable assistance is provided by an individual to offset an official's medical or living expenses
as an "act of human compassion." (Regulation 18942(a)(18)(B).) Recognizing that the exception
allows for unlimited, unreported payments by an individual to a public official, the exception is
limited to particular circumstances and does not extend to gifts when there is any indication that

File No. A-20-005
Page No. 4

gift may be intended to influence officials in their official capacities. Regulation 18942(a)(18)(B) seeks to balance the need to not disadvantage a public official faced with catastrophic needs, while recognizing that the Act's main purpose is to protect public integrity by closely guarding against opportunities for financial influence by individuals in regard to an official with a role in past, current or future government decision-making related to the individual's interests.

Regulation 18942(a)(18)(B) and (D) states:

Payments provided to an official, or an official's family member, by an individual to offset family medical or living expenses that the official can no longer meet without private assistance because of an accident, illness, employment loss, death in the family, or other unexpected calamity; or to defray expenses associated with humanitarian efforts such as the adoption of an orphaned child, so long as the source of the donation is an individual who has a prior social relationship with the official of the type where it would be common to provide such assistance (such as a relative, long-term friend, neighbor, co-worker or former co-worker, member of the same local religious or other similar organization, etc.), or the payment is made without regard to official status under other circumstances in which it would be common to receive community outreach, unless the individual providing the benefit to the official is listed under (D)(i-iii) below.

....

(D)(i) A lobbyist, lobbying firm, lobbyist employer, or other person required to file reports under Chapter 6 (commencing with Section 86100) of the Act and who is registered to lobby the official's agency.

(ii) A person who has, or may reasonably foreseeably have, a contract, license, permit, or other entitlement for use pending before the official's agency, and for 12 months following the date a contract is signed or a final decision is rendered in the proceeding, if the official makes or participates in making a governmental decision, as defined in the Act's conflict of interest regulations (Regulation 18702 et seq. ) regarding the contract, license, permit, or other entitlement for use.

(iii) A person, or an agent of a person, involved in a licensing or enforcement proceeding before a regulatory agency that employs the official and in which the official may reasonably foreseeably participate, or has participated, within 12 months of the time the gift is made.

Based on the facts provided, Mayor William's relationship with the Todd Kaplan and Drew Milburn is related to their Vertical Companies business interest in the City and to Mayor William's official status. They had land use decisions related to their business before the City Council within the past 12 months. Presumably, Mayor Williams was involved in these decisions before the City Council. The exception for payments of "human compassion" does not apply where the person has had entitlement for use decisions before the official's agency within the 12 month time period if the official participated in the decision(s). Additionally, the exception does not apply where it is anticipated that the two individuals may reasonably foreseeably have permit and licensing issues before the official's agency. Therefore, due to the nature of their relationship to the Mayor, their

File No. A-20-005
Page No. 5

business' recent land use decisions before the City, and their anticipated business permit and
licensing issues before the City, the limited exception for payments that are acts of human
compassion is not applicable to these individuals. (Regulation 18942(a)(18)(B) and (D)(ii).) Also,
we note that these individuals do not meet the exception requirements for payments by "long term
friends" or "gifts made because of an existing personal relationship." (Regulation 18942(a)(18)(C)
and (19).)

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Dave Bainbridge
General Counsel

By: L. Karen Harrison
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

LKH:aja

sections.question:
1. May Mayor Jeff Williams accept donations to alleviate medical-related living costs from 
Mr. Todd Kaplan or Mr. Drew Milburn, in excess of $500 under the limited exception to the gift 
limits in Regulation 18942(a)(l 8)(B) for "Acts of Human Compassion?" 
2. If so, as a general matter, what are the Mayor's duties in the event either individual is 
involved in a decision before the City Council?
sections.conclusion:
1. No. Under the facts provided, these individuals are connected to Mayor Williams through 
their business interests within the City, their business has had decisions before the City within the 
past 12 months, and it is anticipated that their business will have business permit and licensing 
issues before the City in the near future. Therefore, any such payments from the individuals would 
not meet the limited exception to the definition of a "gift," under Regulation 18942(a)(l 8)(B) for 
payments that are acts of human compassion unrelated to the official's status. 
2. As a general matter, if Mr. Williams accepts gifts from either individual, the gift limits, 
reporting requirements, and conflict of interest prohibitions will apply, as discussed below. The Act 
prohibits Mayor Williams from accepting any gift over $500, as well as making, participating in 
making, or using his position to influence the outcome of a governmental decision involving the 
donor of a gift or gifts with an aggregate value of $500 or more provided to, received by, or 
promised to him within the 12 months prior to the date the decision is made.

sections.analysis:
A "gift" is a subcategory of "income" (Section 82030) under the Act, and is defined in 
Section 82028(a) as: 
.. . any payment that confers a personal benefit on the recipient, to the extent that 
consideration of equal or greater value is not received and includes a rebate or 
discount in the price of anything of value unless the rebate or discount is made in

status. 
The type of payment you describe from Mr. Kaplan or Mr. Milburn would qualify as a gift 
to Mayor Williams so long as he is serving as a public official. To reduce improper influences on 
public officials, the Act regulates the receipt of gifts by public officials in three ways: 
• 
Gift Limits: The Act places limitations on the acceptance of gifts by certain 
public officials. The current limit is $500, in aggregate, from a single source 
in a calendar year. (Section 89503, Regulation 18940.2.) Gifts from lobbyists 
or a lobbying firm that lobbies the official's agency may not aggregate to 
more than $10 in a calendar month. (Section 86203.) 
• 
Gift Reporting: Any gift of $50 or more (or gifts that aggregate to $50 or 
more) from the same source received in a calendar year must be disclosed on 
the officials' statements of economic interests. (Section 87207.) 
• 
Conflict of Interest Disqualification: The Act prohibits any public official 
from making, participating in making, or using his or her position to influence 
the outcome of a governmental decision involving the donor of a gift or gifts 
with an aggregate value of $500 or more provided to, received by, or 
promised to the official within the 12 months prior to the date the decision is 
made. (Sections 87100 and 87103(e).) 
Unless the payments fall under an exception to the definition a "gift" the payments are

in City decisions involving the individual's financial interests, including a business interest.

gift may be intended to influence officials in their official capacities. Regulation 18942(a)(l 8)(B) 
seeks to balance the need to not disadvantage a public official faced with catastrophic needs, while 
recognizing that the Act's main purpose is to protect public integrity by closely guarding against 
opportunities for financial influence by individuals in regard to an official with a role in past, 
current or future government decision-making related to the individual's interests. 
Regulation 18942(a)(18)(B) and (D) states: 
Payments provided to an official, or an official's family member, by an individual 
to offset family medical or living expenses that the official can no longer meet 
without private assistance because of an accident, illness, employment loss, death 
in the family, or other unexpected calamity; or to defray expenses associated with 
humanitarian efforts such as the adoption of an orphaned child, so long as the 
source of the donation is an individual who has a prior social relationship with the 
official of the type where it would be common to provide such assistance (such as 
a relative, long-term friend, neighbor, co-worker or former co-worker, member of

made without regard to official status under other circumstances in which it would 
be common to receive community outreach, unless the individual providing the 
benefit to the official is listed under (D)(i-iii) below. 
(D)(i) A lobbyist, lobbying firm, lobbyist employer, or other person required to 
file reports under Chapter 6 ( commencing with Section 86100) of the Act and 
who is registered to lobby the official's agency. 
(ii) A person who has, or may reasonably foreseeably have, a contract, license,
permit, or other entitlement for use pending before the official's agency, and for
12 months following the date a contract is signed or a final decision is rendered in
the proceeding, if the official makes or participates in making a governmental
decision, as defined in the Act's conflict of interest regulations (Regulation 18702
et seq.) regarding the contract, license, permit, or other entitlement for use.
(iii) A person, or an agent of a person, involved in a licensing or enforcement
proceeding before a regulatory agency that employs the official and in which the
official may reasonably foreseeably participate, or has participated, within 12

Based on the facts provided, Mayor William's relationship with the Todd Kaplan and Drew 
Milburn is related to their Vertical Companies business interest in the City and to Mayor William's 
official status. They had land use decisions related to their business before the City Council within 
the past 12 months. Presumably, Mayor Williams was involved in these decisions before the City

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1. May Mayor Jeff Williams accept donations to alleviate medical-related living costs from 
Mr. Todd Kaplan or Mr. Drew Milburn, in excess of $500 under the limited exception to the gift 
limits in Regulation 18942(a)(l 8)(B) for "Acts of Human Compassion?" 
2. If so, as a general matter, what are the Mayor's duties in the event either individual is 
involved in a decision before the City Council?

CONCLUSION: 1. No. Under the facts provided, these individuals are connected to Mayor Williams through 
their business interests within the City, their business has had decisions before the City within the 
past 12 months, and it is anticipated that their business will have business permit and licensing 
issues before the City in the near future. Therefore, any such payments from the individuals would 
not meet the limited exception to the definition of a "gift," under Regulation 18942(a)(l 8)(B) for 
payments that are acts of human compassion unrelated to the official's status. 
2. As a general matter, if Mr. Williams accepts gifts from either individual, the gift limits, 
reporting requirements, and conflict of interest prohibitions will apply, as discussed below. The Act 
prohibits Mayor Williams from accepting any gift over $500, as well as making, participating in 
making, or using his position to influence the outcome of a governmental decision involving the 
donor of a gift or gifts with an aggregate value of $500 or more provided to, received by, or 
promised to him within the 12 months prior to the date the decision is made.
